rxn_class_summary: Electrochemical aziridination of olefins using N-aminophthalimide as nitrene precursor under metal-free anodic oxidation conditions
electrochemical_role: Anodic oxidation of N-aminophthalimide to a nitrogen-centered species that transfers to olefin; protons reduced at platinum cathode to H2
electron_flow: At +1.80 V (vs Ag wire) on platinum anode, N-aminophthalimide undergoes one-electron oxidation (CV shows anodic peaks at +1.35 and +1.68 V) generating an N-centered oxidized intermediate that, with assistance of NEt3/AcOH, transfers a nitrene-equivalent to the olefin; protons are reduced at the cathode to H2.

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of N-aminophthalimide
probe_reagents.observation: Two irreversible one-electron oxidations at +1.35 V and +1.68 V vs Ag/AgCl

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of cyclohexene
probe_reagents.observation: Only -1.0 μA anodic current at +1.68 V vs -152 μA for N-aminophthalimide, indicating olefin oxidation is kinetically disfavored (overpotential)

probe_reagents.name: cyclic voltammetry of Pb(OAc)2
probe_reagents.observation: Pb(II)/Pb(IV) oxidation at +1.60 V vs Ag/AgCl, enabling catalytic Pb variant as control

notes: A related Pb(OAc)4-catalyzed (10 mol%) electrochemical variant was demonstrated, where Pb(II) is reoxidized to Pb(IV) at +1.60 V and acts as mediator; the metal-free pathway is favored as the main contribution and proceeds at slightly higher potential (+1.80 V) where direct N-aminophthalimide oxidation dominates over olefin background oxidation. Exact nature of the transferred nitrogen species (free singlet/triplet nitrene vs metal-free aminyl radical) is not definitively assigned.
